provenance.source_corpus: Assessing the long-term ecological effects of riparian management practices on headwater streams in a coastal temperate rainforest

source_spans.text: About 15 years after clear-cutting and 9 years after thinning, slower decomposition of red alder leaves ( Alnus rubra) was reported in streams affected by forest harvesting
About 15 years after clear-cutting and 9 years after thinning, slower decomposition of red alder leaves ( Alnus rubra) was reported in streams affected by forest harvesting

nodes.name: slower decomposition of red alder leaves

source_spans.text: slower decomposition of red alder leaves ( Alnus rubra) was reported in streams affected by forest harvesting
attributed to lower density of shredder invertebrates

After a further 7 years of forest regrowth (time elapsed between the previous and present study), we found that litter decomposition rate and shredder density in logging-affected reaches converged with values in reference reaches
litter decomposition rate and shredder density in logging-affected reaches converged with values in reference reaches
Stream functional integrity, as reﬂected by litter decomposition, increased across all forest treatments
After a 7-year period of forest regrowth subsequent to the study by Lecerf and Richardson (2010), canopy openness declined overall
pH in clear-cut sites (mean: 5.7) was lower than that in 30-m reserves (mean: 6.4; Tukey’s HSD: p = 0.04)

edges.original_sentence: About 15 years after clear-cutting and 9 years after thinning, slower decomposition of red alder leaves ( Alnus rubra) was reported in streams affected by forest harvesting, which was attributed to lower density of shredder invertebrates.
